title: Do not go near the Dead - 2
subtitle: The Nazarite Vow – 8

content:
Continuing from Paul’s cry, the words “wretched man” describe more than sadness. It describes a person worn down by conflict, distressed by a battle that cannot be won by human strength alone. Paul is not praising sin. He is not saying sin is enjoyable. He is saying sin is hateful. He is describing the pain of wanting righteousness while feeling the flesh pulling in another direction. This is why he says, “What I want to do I do not do, but what I hate I do” (Romans 7:15). The wretched man is not the one who loves evil. The wretched man is the one who sees Yahweh’s law is good, desires what is right, yet discovers another desire working in the body; a force of sin fighting against the mind’s desire for obedience (Romans 7:23).

Paul’s question is also important. He asks “Who will free me?” not “What will free me?” This tells us the solution is not merely a technique, not merely stronger discipline, and not merely more rules. The deliverance must come from a deliverer. The answer comes immediately: “I thank God through Jesus Christ our Lord!” (Romans 7:25). Paul does not stay in despair. He turns his eyes to Yahweh’s rescue through Christ.

This is where the Nazirite rule about death becomes bright for New Creation believers. The Nazirite vow was a picture of separation from death and defilement. But Paul shows that the greatest death is not a corpse outside; it is sin’s power inside. He speaks of a “body of death” because sin works through mortal life and tries to drag the whole person into corruption. Paul is not saying the human body itself is evil as Yahweh created it. He is speaking about the sinful nature operating through the body, using desires and impulses as weapons. This is why the Nazirite law, when applied spiritually, calls us to a deeper separation. It calls us not only to avoid outward defilement, but to refuse the inward dominion of sin.

There is an ancient form of punishment where a murderer was tied to a dead corpse and forced to carry it, until the rot and infection slowly killed the living person. Whether every detail of that account can be proven in history or not, the picture itself is clear, and it matches Paul’s language well: carrying death is unbearable, and death spreads corruption.
Sin works like that. It clings. It stinks. It infects. It drains strength. It turns the inner life into a slow decay if it is not judged and removed. This is why Paul cries out. He is describing a life where the will is tired and the mind longs for freedom from death’s grip.

Yahweh’s answer is not to leave us tied to death. Yahweh’s answer is Messiah. “I thank God through Jesus Christ our Lord” is not a religious phrase. It is the doorway to hope. It means Yahweh has provided a real rescue. It means Yahweh does not only command holiness; He also provides deliverance and a new way of life. That is why Romans 8 opens with a strong declaration: “There is therefore now no condemnation to those who are in Christ Jesus” (Romans 8:1). Condemnation is the sentence of the court. It is the end of the road. But Yahweh declares that those who are in Christ are not under that sentence. The believer is not meant to live under crushing guilt, because Yahweh’s mercy has opened a path of freedom.

Romans 8 continues by describing this freedom as being set free from
“the law of sin and death” by “the law of the Spirit of life” (Romans 8:2). The verse itself speaks of life overcoming death. It speaks of a new governing principle. Death ruled when sin ruled. But now life rules where Yahweh’s deliverance rules. This is not a call to treat sin lightly. It is a call to walk in a new power Yahweh gives, so that sin no longer dominates like a master. The Nazirite was told to stay away from the dead. In the New Creation, Yahweh calls us to stay away from the dominion of sin and death, not by human effort alone, but by the deliverance He gives through Messiah.

So what does this mean in daily practice? It means the separated life does not play with dead works. It does not excuse fleshly rage. It does not entertain jealousy. It does not feed pride. It does not keep vengeance warm in the heart. It does not pass gossip as if it is harmless. It does not build groups around bitterness. These are dead works, and dead works defile. Haggai’s warning becomes personal: uncleanness spreads through touch. In the same way, dead works spread through words, attitudes, and agreements. So the believer must learn to say no early. The Nazirite did not even go near the dead. In the same way, we do not go near the first steps of sin. We cut it off while it is still at the doorway.

This is also why the Nazirite law mentions close family. The pressure to compromise often comes through relationships that are dear.
Yet Yahweh’s call remains higher. Even if father, mother, brother, sister, spouse, or close friend tries to pull the heart into dead works, the separated life must choose Yahweh. This is not cruelty. This is loyalty.
It is protecting the vow. It is guarding the new man. It is refusing to be cut off from Yahweh’s fellowship by returning to death.

When we fail, the answer is not to hide in shame and let sin ferment.
The answer is to return quickly. Isaiah said sin cuts off, but the same Yahweh who warns also heals the repentant. The New Creation path is a path of light. In light, confession happens. In light, repentance is not delayed. In light, bitterness is uprooted. In light, the heart is washed and made tender again. And as Yahweh strengthens us, the cry of the wretched man is not the final line. The final line becomes thanksgiving, and the walk becomes freedom.

So the Nazirite rule about death becomes a daily call. Stay away from dead works. Stay away from the seeds of sin. Stay away from the groups formed around bitterness. Stay away from the influences that cut off prayer and harden the conscience. Walk as one who has been called out of death into life. Yahweh has not left us to carry the corpse forever.
He has provided deliverance through Messiah, and He calls us to live in that deliverance with holy fear, clean conscience, and steady obedience.

title: Prayer
text:
Oh God, Deliverer of Your people, thank You for providing rescue through Christ.
Save us from the dominion of sin and death, and keep our steps in the path of life.
Teach us to hate dead works and to refuse them early, before they spread and defile.
Guard our families and our fellowship from bitterness, gossip, and fleshly anger.
Give us grace to repent quickly, and strength to obey with a clean conscience.
May our lives remain set apart to You, walking in light, bearing fruit that pleases You.
In Christ name, amen.
